description: Prediction, prevention and management of threatened and established preterm labour.

sba.stem: A woman presents at 30 weeks with regular painful contractions. Speculum shows no visible cervical change and membranes are intact. Fetal fibronectin is being considered.
sba.lead: What is the main value of a negative fetal fibronectin test here?
options: It has a high positive predictive value for delivery within 48 hours
It has a high negative predictive value, making delivery within 14 days unlikely
It indicates the need for immediate delivery
sba.rationale: Fetal fibronectin has a high negative predictive value: a negative test makes delivery within the next 14 days unlikely and supports avoiding unnecessary admission and intervention.
sba.hook: Negative fFN -> delivery unlikely, strong NPV.

sba.stem: A woman in confirmed preterm labour at 29 weeks is stable and delivery is anticipated within 24 hours.
sba.lead: Which intervention most improves neonatal outcome by reducing respiratory distress syndrome?
sba.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids reduce neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular haemorrhage and mortality, and are given between 24 and 34+6 weeks when preterm birth is expected.
sba.hook: Anticipated preterm birth -> antenatal steroids for RDS.

sba.stem: A woman is in established preterm labour at 30 weeks with delivery expected within hours.
sba.lead: What is the primary purpose of giving intravenous magnesium sulphate?
sba.rationale: Magnesium sulphate given before anticipated early preterm birth (below 30-34 weeks) provides fetal neuroprotection, reducing the risk of cerebral palsy.
sba.hook: Imminent early preterm birth -> MgSO4 for neuroprotection.

sba.stem: A woman at 31 weeks with intact membranes and confirmed preterm labour is being considered for tocolysis to allow steroids and in-utero transfer.
sba.lead: Which agent is a recommended first-line tocolytic?
options: Indometacin as first choice at all gestations
sba.rationale: Nifedipine and atosiban are recommended tocolytics. Tocolysis is used to gain time for corticosteroids and transfer, not to prolong pregnancy indefinitely; magnesium sulphate is not used as a tocolytic.
sba.hook: Tocolysis to buy time -> nifedipine (or atosiban).

sba.stem: A woman with a history of one spontaneous preterm birth at 28 weeks is now 16 weeks pregnant. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a cervical length of 22 mm.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate intervention to reduce preterm birth?
sba.rationale: A short cervix (25 mm or less before 24 weeks) with a history of spontaneous preterm birth warrants either vaginal progesterone or cervical cerclage to reduce recurrent preterm birth.
sba.hook: Prior PTB + short cervix -> progesterone or cerclage.

emq.instruction: For each scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate intervention. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.
options: H. Expectant management with no intervention

stems.stem: A stable woman at 28 weeks with threatened preterm labour, to reduce neonatal RDS if she delivers.
stems.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids reduce RDS and are given when preterm birth is anticipated between 24 and 34+6 weeks.

stems.stem: A woman at 29 weeks now fully dilated and about to deliver, to protect the fetal brain.
stems.rationale: Magnesium sulphate provides fetal neuroprotection when very preterm birth is imminent.
stems.hook: Imminent very preterm birth -> MgSO4 neuroprotection.

stems.stem: An asymptomatic woman at 20 weeks with a previous 26-week birth and cervical length 18 mm.
stems.rationale: A short cervix with prior preterm birth may be managed with cerclage (or vaginal progesterone) to reduce recurrence.
